question: You respond to a 58-year-old male at a restaurant who is complaining of chest discomfort that began 45 minutes ago while eating dinner. He describes it as a pressure or squeezing sensation in the center of his chest that radiates to his left arm and jaw. He is diaphoretic and appears anxious. He has loosened his collar and is rubbing his chest. His wife states he has a history of high blood pressure and high cholesterol and takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. He has never had a heart attack. Vital signs are BP 162/98 HR 94 RR 20 SpO2 96% on room air. What is your initial assessment and what interventions should you perform at the AEMT level?
option_a: This is likely indigestion from eating too fast. Give him antacids and have him rest
option_b: This presentation is highly suspicious for acute coronary syndrome. Give aspirin 324 mg chewed oxygen if SpO2 drops below 94% establish IV access obtain 12-lead ECG if capable and administer nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL if BP permits
option_c: He is having a panic attack and needs reassurance only
option_d: Wait to see if the pain resolves on its own before treating
rationale: This patient has classic signs of acute coronary syndrome including substernal chest pressure radiating to arm and jaw diaphoresis and multiple cardiac risk factors. At AEMT level immediate interventions include aspirin 324 mg chewed to inhibit platelet aggregation supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 is below 94% or patient is dyspneic IV access for potential medication administration 12-lead ECG if trained and equipped and nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual if systolic BP is above 100 and no contraindications. Nitroglycerin can be repeated every 5 minutes up to 3 doses. Rapid transport to a cardiac-capable facility is essential.

question: You are treating the chest pain patient and have given aspirin 324 mg. Before administering nitroglycerin you ask about other medications. The patient states he took sildenafil Viagra last night for erectile dysfunction. His blood pressure is 158/94 and pain is 8 out of 10. Why is this information critical and how does it change your treatment plan?
option_a: Sildenafil does not interact with nitroglycerin so proceed with NTG administration
option_b: Sildenafil and similar erectile dysfunction medications potentiate the hypotensive effects of nitroglycerin. Do NOT give nitroglycerin as it could cause life-threatening hypotension. Continue other ACS treatment and transport rapidly
option_c: You can give half the normal nitroglycerin dose
option_d: The medication interaction only matters if he took it today
rationale: Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors like sildenafil Viagra tadalafil Cialis and vardenafil Levitra cause vasodilation and significantly potentiate the hypotensive effects of nitrates. Concurrent use can cause profound potentially fatal hypotension. Sildenafil and vardenafil are contraindications to nitroglycerin for 24 hours and tadalafil for 48 hours due to its longer half-life. Continue other ACS treatments including aspirin IV access oxygen if indicated and obtain 12-lead ECG. Transport rapidly and notify the hospital so they can prepare alternative treatments for his pain and ischemia.

question: A 67-year-old female presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath and mild chest discomfort that began 2 hours ago. She denies classic crushing chest pain but states she feels like an elephant is sitting on her chest. She has nausea and has vomited once. She appears pale and is diaphoretic. She has a history of diabetes and hypertension. Her medications include metformin glipizide and lisinopril. Vital signs are BP 146/88 HR 102 RR 24 SpO2 93% on room air. Blood glucose is 186 mg/dL. Why should you suspect ACS despite her atypical presentation?
option_a: Her blood sugar is elevated so this is a diabetic emergency not cardiac
option_b: Women and diabetics often have atypical ACS presentations. Her symptoms of dyspnea chest pressure nausea diaphoresis with cardiac risk factors should be treated as ACS. Give aspirin oxygen establish IV and obtain 12-lead ECG
option_c: Since she denies crushing chest pain this is not a heart attack
option_d: She is too young to have a heart attack
rationale: Women diabetics and elderly patients frequently present with atypical symptoms of acute coronary syndrome. Instead of classic crushing substernal chest pain they may have dyspnea fatigue nausea vomiting back pain jaw pain or vague discomfort. Diabetic neuropathy can blunt cardiac pain perception. This patient has multiple risk factors diabetes hypertension older age and symptoms that could represent ACS. Treat her the same as a patient with classic symptoms including aspirin supplemental oxygen for her hypoxia IV access and 12-lead ECG. Her atypical presentation does not make her condition less serious.

question: You have obtained a 12-lead ECG on a 62-year-old male with chest pain. The ECG shows ST segment elevation of 3 mm in leads II III and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL. The patient's blood pressure is 88/58 and he feels lightheaded when sitting up. Before giving nitroglycerin what must you consider about this ECG pattern?
option_a: The low blood pressure alone contraindicates nitroglycerin
option_b: ST elevation in leads II III aVF indicates inferior wall MI which often involves the right ventricle. RV infarction is preload-dependent and nitroglycerin can cause severe hypotension. With his already low BP nitroglycerin is contraindicated. Give IV fluid bolus instead
option_c: This ECG pattern is normal and he does not need treatment
option_d: Give nitroglycerin to improve his blood flow
rationale: ST elevation in leads II III and aVF indicates inferior wall myocardial infarction typically caused by right coronary artery occlusion. The RCA also supplies the right ventricle in most people so inferior MI often involves RV infarction. The right ventricle depends heavily on preload to maintain cardiac output. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation reducing preload which can cause severe hypotension and cardiovascular collapse in RV infarction. This patient already has hypotension making nitroglycerin especially dangerous. Instead give IV normal saline bolus to maintain preload. Right-sided ECG leads particularly V4R can confirm RV involvement if you are trained to obtain them.

question: You obtain a 12-lead ECG on a 55-year-old male with crushing chest pain. The ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1 V2 V3 and V4 with reciprocal depression in the inferior leads. What does this indicate and what is the significance for the patient?
option_a: This is a normal ECG variant and not concerning
option_b: This shows anterior wall STEMI involving the left anterior descending artery. This is a large territory MI with significant mortality risk. Activate the cath lab early and transport immediately to a PCI-capable hospital
option_c: This indicates the patient is having a panic attack
option_d: Wait to get a second ECG to confirm
rationale: ST elevation in the precordial leads V1-V4 indicates anterior wall myocardial infarction caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending LAD artery. The LAD supplies a large territory including the anterior wall of the left ventricle and the anterior interventricular septum. Anterior MIs carry higher mortality than inferior MIs due to the larger amount of myocardium at risk. Early recognition allows activation of the cardiac catheterization lab so the team is ready when the patient arrives. Time is muscle and every minute of delay increases myocardial damage. Transport rapidly to a PCI-capable facility.

question: A 72-year-old female with a history of angina presents with chest pain similar to her usual angina. She took one of her own nitroglycerin tablets 10 minutes ago without relief. Her pain is now 7 out of 10. Vital signs are BP 148/88 HR 82 RR 18 SpO2 97% on room air. She denies taking any erectile dysfunction medications and has no other contraindications. You have given aspirin. What is the appropriate approach to nitroglycerin administration?
option_a: She already took nitroglycerin so you should not give more
option_b: Administer nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual. You can give up to 3 doses total including her home dose at 5-minute intervals as long as systolic BP remains above 100 and she has no contraindications
option_c: Give her three nitroglycerin tablets at once for faster relief
option_d: Nitroglycerin is not appropriate for angina only for heart attacks
rationale: Nitroglycerin causes coronary vasodilation and venodilation reducing cardiac workload and improving blood flow to ischemic myocardium. It can be administered every 5 minutes up to 3 total doses for ongoing chest pain as long as systolic blood pressure remains above 100 mmHg and there are no contraindications. Her home dose counts toward the total. Monitor blood pressure before each dose. If pain persists after 3 doses and BP remains adequate this suggests more severe ischemia or infarction rather than stable angina. Continue monitoring and transport. Document response to each nitroglycerin dose.

question: After the second nitroglycerin dose the angina patient states her pain is now 3 out of 10 much improved. However her blood pressure has dropped from 148/88 to 96/62 and she feels dizzy. What should you do?
option_a: Give a third nitroglycerin to eliminate the remaining pain
option_b: Do not give additional nitroglycerin due to the hypotension. Lay her supine elevate her legs and consider a small IV fluid bolus. Monitor closely and be prepared if blood pressure drops further
option_c: Her pain improved so the blood pressure drop is acceptable
option_d: Give her aspirin to raise her blood pressure
rationale: Hypotension is a side effect of nitroglycerin due to its vasodilatory effects. A systolic blood pressure below 100 mmHg is a contraindication to additional nitroglycerin. Lay the patient supine and elevate her legs to improve venous return and blood pressure. A small IV fluid bolus 250 mL may help restore blood pressure. Continue to monitor her closely. Her chest pain has improved significantly which is the goal but you cannot give more nitroglycerin with this blood pressure. If her blood pressure does not improve or she develops signs of shock further interventions may be needed. Document the blood pressure drop and your response.

question: A 68-year-old male presents with palpitations and lightheadedness that started suddenly 1 hour ago. He has no chest pain. He has a history of hypertension. Vital signs are BP 108/72 HR 178 regular RR 18 SpO2 98% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 178 bpm without visible P waves. What is the most likely rhythm and what is the AEMT role in management?
option_a: This is ventricular tachycardia and he needs immediate defibrillation
option_b: This is most likely supraventricular tachycardia SVT. At AEMT level provide supportive care IV access monitoring consider vagal maneuvers if trained and protocolized and prepare for transport. Adenosine is typically a paramedic-level intervention
option_c: This is normal sinus tachycardia from anxiety
option_d: His heart rate is not fast enough to cause symptoms
rationale: A regular narrow complex tachycardia at 178 bpm without visible P waves is most consistent with supraventricular tachycardia likely AVNRT or AVRT. The patient is hemodynamically stable with adequate blood pressure and only mild symptoms. At AEMT level your role includes establishing IV access continuous cardiac monitoring and considering vagal maneuvers such as Valsalva or modified Valsalva if these are within your protocols and training. Vagal maneuvers can terminate SVT by increasing parasympathetic tone. Adenosine administration is typically at the paramedic level. Monitor for deterioration and transport for definitive treatment.

question: Your SVT patient suddenly becomes pale and diaphoretic. His blood pressure drops to 76/50 and he states he feels like he might pass out. He is now confused. The rhythm remains narrow complex tachycardia at 182 bpm. What has changed and what is now indicated?
option_a: Continue monitoring and transport as he is still stable
option_b: He has become hemodynamically unstable with hypotension and altered mental status. Unstable tachycardia requires synchronized cardioversion which is ALS intervention. Provide supportive care call for ALS backup if available and transport rapidly
option_c: Give nitroglycerin to improve his blood flow
option_d: This is a normal response to SVT
rationale: This patient has transitioned from stable to unstable tachycardia. Signs of hemodynamic instability include hypotension altered mental status syncope chest pain and signs of shock. Unstable tachycardia requires immediate intervention to restore perfusion. Synchronized cardioversion is the treatment for unstable narrow complex tachycardia and is typically a paramedic-level intervention. At AEMT level provide supportive care including IV access oxygen and positioning. Call for ALS intercept if available. If the patient loses pulses this becomes pulseless VT or VF requiring defibrillation. Prepare for possible cardiac arrest and transport rapidly.

question: A 75-year-old female presents with fatigue and dizziness for the past day. She nearly passed out this morning. She has a history of heart disease and takes metoprolol and lisinopril. Vital signs are BP 94/58 HR 42 regular RR 16 SpO2 96% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows a regular rhythm at 42 bpm with narrow QRS complexes. P waves are visible and each P wave is followed by a QRS. What is this rhythm and how should you manage this patient?
option_a: This is sinus bradycardia with symptoms. Provide supportive care with IV fluids position supine with legs elevated and monitor closely. Atropine and pacing are ALS interventions
option_b: This is a heart block requiring immediate CPR
option_c: Her heart rate is normal for an elderly patient on metoprolol
option_d: Give nitroglycerin to increase her heart rate
rationale: This is sinus bradycardia at 42 bpm which is slow. The consistent P waves each followed by a QRS indicates the rhythm originates in the SA node and conducts normally just at a slow rate. This patient is symptomatic with hypotension near-syncope and fatigue making this symptomatic bradycardia. Her metoprolol a beta-blocker may be contributing to the slow rate. At AEMT level provide supportive care including IV access IV fluid bolus for hypotension positioning supine with legs elevated to improve perfusion and continuous monitoring. Atropine and transcutaneous pacing are ALS interventions. Transport with monitoring.

question: A 70-year-old male presents with weakness and confusion. His wife says he has been deteriorating over the past few hours. He has a pacemaker implanted 5 years ago. Vital signs are BP 82/54 HR 35 RR 18 SpO2 94% on room air. The cardiac monitor shows pacer spikes followed by QRS complexes at a rate of 35 but you also see pacer spikes that are NOT followed by QRS complexes. What pacemaker malfunction is occurring and how should you manage this?
option_a: The pacemaker is working normally just at a slow rate
option_b: This is pacemaker failure to capture where some pacer spikes are not causing cardiac contraction. The patient is symptomatic. Provide supportive care with IV fluids and positioning and transport urgently. External pacing may be needed as a bridge
option_c: Turn off the pacemaker with a magnet
option_d: This is not a pacemaker problem
rationale: Failure to capture occurs when the pacemaker generates an electrical impulse pacer spike but the impulse does not cause myocardial depolarization resulting in no QRS complex following some spikes. Causes include lead dislodgement or fracture battery depletion increased capture threshold from fibrosis or medications and electrolyte abnormalities. This patient is symptomatic with hypotension altered mental status and inadequate heart rate. At AEMT level provide supportive care including IV access IV fluid bolus and positioning. Transcutaneous pacing may override the failing pacemaker and is an ALS intervention. Transport urgently for pacemaker evaluation and possible replacement.

question: A 78-year-old female is found unresponsive by her husband. She has no pulse and is not breathing. You attach the cardiac monitor while your partner begins CPR. The monitor shows a flat line in the lead you are viewing. Before confirming asystole what must you verify?
option_a: The flat line confirms asystole. Begin ACLS protocol
option_b: Verify asystole by checking in at least two different leads ensuring leads are properly connected checking monitor gain and confirming this is not fine ventricular fibrillation
option_c: A flat line always means the patient is dead
option_d: Check her blood sugar before doing anything else
rationale: Before accepting asystole you must rule out technical issues and fine VF which can appear as a flat line. Verify in at least two different leads by changing lead selection on the monitor. Confirm all leads are properly connected to both the monitor and the patient. Check that the gain is turned up appropriately. Fine VF may appear as a flat line in one lead but show fibrillation in another. True asystole confirmed in multiple leads with proper connections has a very poor prognosis. If confirmed asystole begin CPR and ACLS protocols including epinephrine and addressing reversible causes. Asystole is not a shockable rhythm.

question: You arrive at a residence where a 62-year-old male collapsed 3 minutes ago. His wife called 911 and has been performing CPR as instructed by dispatch. You find him unresponsive and pulseless. The AED is attached and advises shock. What are your immediate priorities and actions in managing this cardiac arrest at the AEMT level?
option_a: Wait for paramedics to arrive before doing anything
option_b: Deliver the shock immediately then resume high-quality CPR. Establish IV or IO access and administer epinephrine 1 mg per protocol. Continue cycles of CPR rhythm analysis and shocks as indicated. Prepare airway equipment
option_c: Check his blood sugar first
option_d: Start CPR and wait 2 minutes before analyzing the rhythm
rationale: In cardiac arrest immediate priorities are high-quality CPR and early defibrillation for shockable rhythms. Deliver the shock immediately then resume CPR for 2 minutes before the next rhythm check. At AEMT level you can establish IV or IO access and administer epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes per your protocols. Maintain high-quality CPR with minimal interruptions compression rate 100-120 per minute depth 2-2.4 inches allowing full chest recoil. Provide BVM ventilation with high-flow oxygen. Prepare for advanced airway if BVM is inadequate. AEMTs play a critical role in the chain of survival.

question: You are managing the cardiac arrest patient. After the second shock and 2 minutes of high-quality CPR the rhythm changes from ventricular fibrillation to an organized narrow complex rhythm at 72 bpm. You stop CPR to check for a pulse. You feel a weak carotid pulse and the patient has some spontaneous agonal respirations at 6 per minute. BP is 68/42. What has occurred and what are your priorities now?
option_a: Resume CPR because the blood pressure is too low
option_b: The patient has achieved return of spontaneous circulation ROSC. Do not resume CPR if pulse is present. Priorities are airway management ventilation at 10-12 per minute oxygenation and blood pressure support with IV fluids. Continue monitoring for rearrest
option_c: The rhythm is PEA and you should continue CPR
option_d: Terminate resuscitation since his breathing is inadequate
rationale: Return of spontaneous circulation ROSC is indicated by a perfusing rhythm with palpable pulse. This patient has ROSC despite his low blood pressure and inadequate breathing. Do not perform CPR on a patient with a pulse. Post-ROSC priorities include airway management with continued BVM ventilation at 10-12 breaths per minute targeting SpO2 94-98% blood pressure support with IV crystalloid bolus targeting SBP greater than 90 mmHg and continuous monitoring as rearrest is common in the early post-ROSC period. Transport to a facility capable of post-cardiac arrest care including potential cardiac catheterization and targeted temperature management.

question: During transport your post-ROSC patient suddenly becomes pulseless again. The monitor shows ventricular fibrillation. What do you do?
option_a: Continue transport without intervention since he already arrested once
option_b: Immediately defibrillate and resume CPR. Rearrest is common after ROSC and requires immediate intervention. Continue resuscitation efforts during transport
option_c: Pronounce the patient dead since he arrested twice
option_d: Check his blood glucose
rationale: Rearrest after ROSC is common occurring in up to 50% of patients initially resuscitated. When rearrest occurs immediate defibrillation and CPR are required just as with the initial arrest. Do not give up on a patient who rearrests. Defibrillate immediately for VF resume high-quality CPR and continue resuscitation efforts. Epinephrine and other interventions should continue per ACLS protocols. Patients can have multiple episodes of arrest and ROSC. Continue aggressive resuscitation during transport. The patient achieved ROSC once indicating a potentially salvageable heart and should be given every chance at survival.

question: You respond to a cardiac arrest in a 55-year-old male who was found in a bathtub. The bathroom is cold and the water in the tub is cold. He is unresponsive and pulseless. His body temperature feels very cold to touch. CPR is initiated. What modifications to resuscitation should you consider for a hypothermic cardiac arrest?
option_a: Hypothermic patients cannot be resuscitated so do not attempt CPR
option_b: Hypothermic patients may appear dead but can have excellent neurological recovery even after prolonged arrest. Continue CPR handle gently and if in VF defibrillate once then focus on CPR and rewarming. Medications may be ineffective when cold
option_c: Use hot water bottles on his chest during CPR
option_d: Pronounce death immediately due to the cold exposure
rationale: Hypothermic cardiac arrest requires modified resuscitation. The saying you are not dead until you are warm and dead reflects the neuroprotective effects of hypothermia. Patients have survived with good neurological outcomes after prolonged arrest in severe hypothermia. Handle the patient gently as rough movement can trigger arrhythmias in the hypothermic heart. If in VF defibrillate once but if unsuccessful defer additional shocks until temperature rises above 86F or 30C as the cold heart is resistant to defibrillation. Continue high-quality CPR. Medications are less effective when cold. Focus on rewarming and transport to a facility capable of active core rewarming.

question: A 34-year-old pregnant female at approximately 32 weeks gestation collapses and is found to be pulseless. CPR is initiated. What modification is essential when performing CPR on a pregnant patient in the third trimester?
option_a: No modifications are needed for pregnant patients
option_b: Perform CPR with manual left uterine displacement by having someone push the uterus to the left. The gravid uterus compresses the IVC when supine reducing venous return and making standard CPR less effective
option_c: Perform CPR with the patient on her left side only
option_d: CPR is contraindicated in pregnancy
rationale: In late pregnancy the gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava when the patient is supine reducing venous return by up to 30%. This makes standard CPR less effective because cardiac output during compressions depends on adequate venous return. Manual left uterine displacement is performed by having one rescuer push the uterus continuously to the patient's left side while CPR is performed on a firm flat surface. This moves the uterus off the IVC improving venous return. All standard ACLS medications and defibrillation are indicated in pregnancy. If no ROSC occurs within 4-5 minutes perimortem cesarean delivery may be indicated to relieve aortocaval compression.

question: A 68-year-old male with a history of heart failure presents with worsening shortness of breath over the past 3 days. He cannot lie flat and has been sleeping in a recliner. He has bilateral lower extremity edema that is worse than his baseline. He admits to eating salty foods and missing some doses of his diuretic. Vital signs are BP 162/94 HR 96 RR 26 SpO2 90% on room air. Lung sounds reveal crackles in both lower lung fields. What type of heart failure exacerbation is this and what is appropriate AEMT treatment?
option_a: He has a respiratory infection and needs antibiotics
option_b: This is acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary edema from fluid overload. Treatment includes sitting him upright high-flow oxygen or CPAP if available and in your scope and nitroglycerin if blood pressure supports it
option_c: He has pneumonia not heart failure
option_d: Give him IV fluids for dehydration
rationale: This patient has classic signs of acute decompensated heart failure with orthopnea inability to lie flat pulmonary edema crackles elevated JVP peripheral edema and identified triggers dietary indiscretion and diuretic non-compliance. The fluid overload has caused pulmonary edema impairing oxygenation. Treatment focuses on reducing preload and improving oxygenation. Sit him upright to reduce venous return. Provide high-flow oxygen or CPAP if available as positive pressure improves oxygenation and reduces preload. Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL causes venodilation reducing preload and is appropriate if SBP is above 100. Do NOT give IV fluids as this would worsen fluid overload.

question: You are treating the heart failure patient with CPAP at 10 cmH2O. His SpO2 has improved from 90% to 94% and he says he can breathe easier. You have established IV access and given nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL. His blood pressure was 162/94 and is now 138/86. He remains in moderate distress. Should you give additional nitroglycerin?
option_a: No because his blood pressure has already dropped 24 mmHg
option_b: Yes if his chest pain or dyspnea persists and SBP remains above 100 you can give additional nitroglycerin every 5 minutes to further reduce preload and improve his symptoms
option_c: Give morphine instead of more nitroglycerin
option_d: He needs diuretics not more nitroglycerin
rationale: Nitroglycerin reduces preload through venodilation which is beneficial in pulmonary edema. It can be given every 5 minutes as long as systolic BP remains above 100 mmHg and the patient continues to have symptoms. His blood pressure drop of 24 mmHg is expected and acceptable since he remains above 100 systolic. Continue nitroglycerin if dyspnea persists. Monitor blood pressure closely before each dose. CPAP should be continued as it provides ongoing benefit. Diuretics like furosemide are important in CHF management but are typically hospital treatments. The combination of positioning oxygen/CPAP and nitroglycerin is appropriate AEMT-level treatment for acute pulmonary edema.

question: A 74-year-old female with heart failure presents with severe respiratory distress. She is tripoding coughing up pink frothy sputum and can only speak one word at a time. She is pale and diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 88/54 HR 128 RR 36 SpO2 78% on room air. She has crackles throughout both lung fields. Her skin is cool and clammy. How does her presentation differ from the previous patient and how does this change your management?
option_a: She has the same condition and should be treated identically
option_b: She has cardiogenic shock in addition to pulmonary edema evidenced by her hypotension and poor perfusion. Nitroglycerin and CPAP may worsen hypotension. Focus on oxygenation with BVM if needed positioning and rapid transport. Avoid preload reduction in hypotensive patients
option_c: Give aggressive nitroglycerin to reduce her fluid overload
option_d: Give her IV fluids to raise her blood pressure
rationale: This patient has cardiogenic pulmonary edema like the previous patient but she is also in cardiogenic shock with hypotension cool clammy skin and poor perfusion. Cardiogenic shock means the heart cannot pump adequately to meet tissue needs. In this situation preload-reducing treatments like nitroglycerin and CPAP may worsen hypotension by further reducing cardiac filling. CPAP also decreases venous return. Focus on oxygenation with high-flow oxygen or BVM assistance if needed positioning to optimize her work of breathing and rapid transport. She needs advanced interventions such as inotropes that are beyond AEMT scope. This is a critically ill patient.

question: A 52-year-old male presents with severe headache blurred vision and chest discomfort. He has a history of hypertension but admits he stopped taking his medications 3 months ago. He appears anxious and uncomfortable. Vital signs are BP 224/138 HR 102 RR 20 SpO2 97% on room air. He has no focal neurological deficits. What is this condition and what is appropriate AEMT management?
option_a: His blood pressure is only mildly elevated and does not need treatment
option_b: This is hypertensive emergency with symptoms suggesting end-organ damage headache visual changes chest discomfort. At AEMT level provide supportive care IV access cardiac monitoring and transport for BP management. If he has chest pain consistent with ACS consider nitroglycerin which may also help lower BP
option_c: Give him his home blood pressure medication
option_d: This is just anxiety and will resolve if he calms down
rationale: Hypertensive emergency is severely elevated blood pressure with evidence of acute end-organ damage. Target organs include the brain headache visual changes stroke heart chest pain MI and kidneys. This patient has symptoms suggesting brain and possibly cardiac involvement. At AEMT level treatment is primarily supportive with IV access monitoring and transport. If chest pain is consistent with ACS standard ACS treatment including nitroglycerin may be appropriate which would also help reduce blood pressure. Do not give oral home medications as their onset is unpredictable. Definitive treatment with IV antihypertensives is a hospital intervention. Monitor closely for deterioration especially stroke.

question: A 78-year-old female is found on the floor of her bathroom by her husband. He heard a thud and found her lying next to the toilet. She is now awake and says she felt lightheaded while urinating and the next thing she remembers is being on the floor. She denies chest pain or palpitations. She has a history of hypertension and takes metoprolol and lisinopril. Vital signs lying down are BP 132/78 HR 68 RR 16 SpO2 98%. What type of syncope is most likely and what additional assessment is important?
option_a: This is definitely a cardiac arrhythmia and she needs a pacemaker
option_b: This is most likely vasovagal or situational syncope triggered by the vagal response to urination. Obtain orthostatic vital signs by checking BP and HR lying then sitting then standing. Assess for injuries from the fall
option_c: She had a stroke
option_d: Her medications caused the syncope
rationale: Vasovagal syncope is the most common cause of syncope and can be triggered by situations like urination called micturition syncope straining or emotional stress. The vagal response causes bradycardia and vasodilation leading to transient cerebral hypoperfusion and loss of consciousness. Orthostatic vital signs are important to check for orthostatic hypotension which could contribute. Check BP and HR lying sitting and standing with a drop of 20 mmHg systolic or 10 mmHg diastolic being significant. Also assess for injuries from the fall as elderly patients are prone to fractures. Although this sounds like vasovagal syncope cardiac causes must be ruled out in elderly patients so transport for evaluation is appropriate.

question: You have obtained orthostatic vital signs on the syncope patient. Lying BP is 132/78 HR 68. Sitting BP is 126/76 HR 72. Standing BP is 88/54 HR 120 and she feels lightheaded and needs to sit down. What do these findings indicate?
option_a: Her vital signs are normal and she can refuse transport
option_b: She has significant orthostatic hypotension with a greater than 20 mmHg drop in systolic BP on standing. This combined with her age and medications metoprolol lisinopril suggests she needs evaluation for causes of orthostatic hypotension and is at high risk for recurrent syncope and falls
option_c: The standing heart rate increase shows she is just anxious
option_d: Only the lying blood pressure matters
rationale: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a drop of 20 mmHg or more in systolic BP or 10 mmHg in diastolic BP within 3 minutes of standing. This patient has a 44 mmHg drop in systolic BP which is significant. Her heart rate appropriately increases trying to compensate but is inadequate to maintain blood pressure. Contributing factors likely include her medications as both beta-blockers and ACE inhibitors can contribute to orthostatic hypotension and possibly dehydration. She is at high risk for recurrent syncope and falls. Transport for evaluation and potentially medication adjustment. She should not be left alone and certainly should not drive.

question: A 68-year-old male had witnessed syncope while walking. Bystanders report he suddenly collapsed without warning and was unconscious for about 30 seconds. They report he appeared pale and may have had brief twitching movements. He has no history of seizures. He is now alert and oriented but tired. He denies any warning symptoms before passing out. Vital signs are normal and he is in normal sinus rhythm on the monitor. What feature of his syncope is concerning and why should he be transported?
option_a: Since his vital signs are normal and he feels fine he can refuse transport
option_b: Syncope without warning or prodrome especially during exertion or while upright and walking suggests cardiac syncope possibly from arrhythmia or structural heart disease. Cardiac syncope carries risk of sudden death. He needs cardiac evaluation
option_c: The brief twitching confirms this was a seizure not syncope
option_d: He had vasovagal syncope and will be fine
rationale: Cardiac syncope is suggested by several features including lack of prodrome or warning syncope during exertion or while upright syncope in older patients or those with heart disease and brief clonic movements which can occur with any cerebral hypoperfusion and do not indicate seizure. Vasovagal syncope typically has prodromal symptoms like nausea warmth tunnel vision. This patient had sudden collapse without warning while walking which raises concern for arrhythmia such as ventricular tachycardia or heart block or structural heart disease. Cardiac syncope carries significant risk of sudden death. He needs cardiac evaluation including ECG monitoring and possibly echocardiogram.

question: A 62-year-old male presents with sudden onset of severe pain in his right leg that began 2 hours ago. His right leg is pale and cool compared to the left. He cannot feel you touching his right foot and cannot move his toes. You cannot palpate a dorsalis pedis or posterior tibial pulse on the right. The left leg has normal pulses and sensation. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and heart failure. His medications include warfarin diltiazem and furosemide. What is the likely diagnosis and why is his atrial fibrillation relevant?
option_a: He has a muscle cramp from dehydration
option_b: This is acute arterial occlusion likely from an embolus originating from his fibrillating left atrium. The 6 Ps of acute limb ischemia are present Pain Pallor Pulselessness Paresthesias Paralysis and Poikilothermia. This is a surgical emergency requiring rapid transport
option_c: He has deep vein thrombosis
option_d: His leg fell asleep from sitting too long
rationale: Acute arterial occlusion is a limb-threatening emergency. The classic 6 Ps are Pain Pallor Pulselessness Paresthesias abnormal sensation Paralysis and Poikilothermia cool temperature. This patient has all six. Atrial fibrillation causes blood stasis in the left atrium promoting thrombus formation. These thrombi can embolize to any arterial bed with the legs being a common site. Acute limb ischemia can lead to irreversible muscle and nerve damage within 6 hours. This patient needs emergent surgical intervention either embolectomy or catheter-based intervention. At AEMT level protect the limb keep it in dependent position avoid pressure or trauma and transport immediately.

question: A 55-year-old female presents with left leg pain and swelling that has been worsening over the past 3 days. She recently returned from a long international flight. Her left calf is visibly swollen red and warm compared to the right. She has pain when you press on the calf. She denies chest pain or shortness of breath. Vital signs are normal. What condition do you suspect and what serious complication must you warn her about?
option_a: She has a muscle strain from the long flight
option_b: This is likely deep vein thrombosis DVT. The serious complication is pulmonary embolism if the clot dislodges and travels to the lungs. Do not massage the leg and advise her that any sudden shortness of breath or chest pain requires immediate emergency care
option_c: She has an infection requiring antibiotics
option_d: Apply ice and elevate the leg
rationale: Deep vein thrombosis DVT occurs when blood clots form in the deep veins usually of the legs. Risk factors include recent prolonged immobility such as long flights surgery malignancy and clotting disorders. Signs include unilateral leg swelling warmth redness and pain. The greatest danger is pulmonary embolism if the clot dislodges and travels through the venous system to the pulmonary arteries. Do not massage the affected leg as this could dislodge the clot. Counsel the patient that any sudden shortness of breath chest pain or lightheadedness requires immediate emergency evaluation for PE. Transport for evaluation including ultrasound to confirm DVT and anticoagulation treatment.

question: A 45-year-old male was involved in a motorcycle crash with significant road rash and a deformed left femur. He is alert but anxious and restless. His skin is pale cool and diaphoretic. Vital signs are BP 92/68 HR 128 RR 24 SpO2 96% on room air. Capillary refill is 4 seconds. He is complaining of thirst. What type of shock is this patient experiencing and what stage?
option_a: He is in compensated hypovolemic shock. The tachycardia pallor diaphoresis and restlessness are compensatory mechanisms trying to maintain blood pressure. His BP is low-normal indicating early decompensation
option_b: He is in cardiogenic shock from a heart attack
option_c: He is in neurogenic shock from spinal injury
option_d: His vital signs are normal and he is not in shock
rationale: This patient is in hypovolemic shock from blood loss likely from his femur fracture and possibly internal injuries. Signs of compensated shock include tachycardia as the heart tries to maintain cardiac output peripheral vasoconstriction causing cool pale skin and delayed capillary refill diaphoresis from sympathetic activation and altered mental status such as anxiety and restlessness. Thirst is a primitive response to volume loss. His blood pressure is being maintained in the low-normal range by these compensatory mechanisms. Without treatment he will progress to decompensated shock with falling blood pressure. Management includes controlling obvious hemorrhage IV access fluid resuscitation per trauma protocols and rapid transport.

question: You continue treating the trauma patient. En route to the hospital his level of consciousness decreases and he becomes confused and lethargic. His blood pressure has dropped to 72/50 and his heart rate has increased to 142. His skin is now mottled and cool throughout. Capillary refill is greater than 5 seconds. What has happened and what is his prognosis without intervention?
option_a: He is just tired from the accident
option_b: He has progressed from compensated to decompensated hypovolemic shock. His compensatory mechanisms have failed and blood pressure has dropped. Without aggressive treatment including hemorrhage control fluids and likely blood transfusion and surgery he will die
option_c: His blood pressure is improving
option_d: Give him nitroglycerin to help his perfusion
rationale: Decompensated shock occurs when the body's compensatory mechanisms fail and can no longer maintain adequate blood pressure and tissue perfusion. Signs include falling blood pressure worsening mental status from lethargy to unresponsiveness mottled or cyanotic skin and prolonged capillary refill. This represents massive volume loss likely greater than 30-40% of blood volume. Without aggressive intervention including hemorrhage control IV fluids blood transfusion and surgical repair of bleeding sources the patient will progress to irreversible shock and death. Continue IV fluid boluses transport immediately and notify the receiving facility of a critical trauma patient.

question: A 58-year-old male presents with severe weakness. He has a history of a GI bleed last year. He states he has had black tarry stools for the past 2 days and vomited blood this morning. He is lethargic and confused. His skin is pale and cool. Vital signs are BP 78/52 HR 136 RR 26 SpO2 95% on room air. What type of shock is this and what should you suspect as the cause?
option_a: This is septic shock from an infection
option_b: This is hypovolemic shock from GI hemorrhage. The black tarry stools melena indicate upper GI bleeding and the hematemesis confirms active bleeding. This is life-threatening hemorrhage requiring aggressive fluid resuscitation and rapid transport
option_c: He is dehydrated from not drinking enough
option_d: His blood pressure is only slightly low
rationale: This patient has hemorrhagic shock from gastrointestinal bleeding. Melena black tarry stools indicates upper GI bleeding where blood is digested as it passes through the intestines. Hematemesis vomiting blood confirms significant upper GI hemorrhage. Common causes include peptic ulcer disease esophageal varices and gastritis. His vital signs indicate severe shock. At AEMT level establish two large-bore IVs give crystalloid fluid boluses and transport rapidly. This patient will likely need blood transfusion and possibly endoscopy or surgery to control bleeding. Position supine with legs elevated if tolerated. Monitor closely for deterioration.

question: A 72-year-old female presents with fever and confusion. Her family states she has had a cough and urinary symptoms for several days. She is warm to touch with flushed skin. Vital signs are BP 86/52 HR 118 RR 24 SpO2 94% Temp 102.8F. Her extremities are warm and well-perfused initially but you notice her mental status is deteriorating and her skin is becoming mottled. What type of shock is this and what is the initial treatment?
option_a: This is hypovolemic shock and she needs IV fluids only
option_b: This is septic shock from infection likely UTI progressing to sepsis. Initial treatment includes IV access aggressive crystalloid fluid bolus 30 mL/kg and rapid transport. Early fluids improve outcomes in sepsis
option_c: She just has the flu and needs rest
option_d: Her fever is causing the low blood pressure and it will improve with cooling
rationale: Septic shock is distributive shock caused by systemic infection leading to widespread vasodilation cytokine release and capillary leak. Early warm septic shock may have warm flushed skin from vasodilation but as shock progresses skin becomes mottled and cool. This patient has identified infection sources UTI and pneumonia with systemic inflammatory response fever tachycardia tachypnea and organ dysfunction hypotension altered mental status meeting criteria for septic shock. Initial treatment per Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus within the first hour. Early fluids improve outcomes. Transport for antibiotics and possible vasopressors.

question: A 64-year-old male presents with sudden onset of severe tearing chest pain that radiates to his back between his shoulder blades. The pain began 1 hour ago and he rates it 10 out of 10. He has a history of long-standing poorly controlled hypertension. Vital signs are BP 188/108 in the right arm and 142/88 in the left arm. HR is 102 RR 22 SpO2 96%. What critical diagnosis must you consider and what finding supports it?
option_a: This is a heart attack and should be treated with aspirin and nitroglycerin
option_b: The tearing pain radiating to the back and significant blood pressure differential greater than 20 mmHg between arms suggest aortic dissection. This is a life-threatening emergency. Do NOT give aspirin or anticoagulants. Gentle transport and pain control with rapid transport to a facility capable of definitive management
option_c: He has a muscle strain from exercise
option_d: The blood pressure difference is a measurement error
rationale: Aortic dissection occurs when a tear in the aortic intima allows blood to dissect between the layers of the aortic wall. Classic presentation includes sudden severe tearing or ripping pain often radiating to the back. Blood pressure differential greater than 20 mmHg between arms suggests the dissection involves the subclavian artery origin. This is a surgical emergency with high mortality. Do NOT give aspirin thrombolytics or anticoagulants as these could be fatal. Treatment focuses on heart rate and blood pressure control to reduce shear forces on the aorta. Transport gently and rapidly to a facility with cardiothoracic surgery capability.

question: You suspect aortic dissection in the patient with tearing chest pain and blood pressure differential. His pain is severe. He is requesting pain medication. Your protocols include fentanyl for pain management. Is it appropriate to give pain medication and why might it be beneficial beyond comfort?
option_a: Pain medication is never appropriate for chest pain
option_b: Yes pain medication is appropriate and beneficial. Severe pain causes catecholamine release which increases heart rate and blood pressure both of which worsen aortic dissection by increasing shear forces on the damaged aorta. Pain control helps limit these forces
option_c: Give aspirin instead of narcotics for chest pain
option_d: Wait until the hospital to give any medication
rationale: Pain management in aortic dissection is not just for patient comfort but is therapeutically important. Severe pain triggers catecholamine release causing tachycardia and hypertension. In aortic dissection elevated heart rate and blood pressure increase shear forces on the aortic wall potentially extending the dissection. Pain control with IV opioids like fentanyl helps reduce these sympathetic responses. The goal is to maintain heart rate below 60 bpm and systolic BP between 100-120 mmHg which typically requires IV beta-blockers at the hospital. At AEMT level providing pain relief with available analgesics helps achieve some of this benefit while transporting.

question: A 56-year-old male is having chest pain consistent with ACS. You have given aspirin 324 mg and are preparing to give nitroglycerin. Before giving nitroglycerin what questions should you ask and what vital sign must you check?
option_a: No questions are needed before giving nitroglycerin
option_b: Ask about use of erectile dysfunction medications in the past 24-48 hours right ventricular infarction symptoms and check that systolic BP is above 100 mmHg. Also ask about sensitivity to nitrates and use of other medications
option_c: Just check if he has taken nitroglycerin before
option_d: Only check blood pressure after giving nitroglycerin
rationale: Before nitroglycerin administration verify: 1 Systolic BP is above 100 mmHg as NTG causes vasodilation and can drop BP further 2 No use of phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors sildenafil vardenafil tadalafil within 24-48 hours as the combination can cause profound hypotension 3 No signs of right ventricular infarction inferior MI with hypotension JVD clear lungs as RV infarction is preload-dependent 4 No known sensitivity to nitrates. Also consider recent use of other vasodilating medications. These contraindications can be life-threatening if ignored.

question: You are treating a 62-year-old female with SVT. Your protocols allow you to assist the patient with vagal maneuvers. Describe the modified Valsalva maneuver and explain why it works.
option_a: Have her drink cold water quickly
option_b: Have her blow into a syringe or bear down as if having a bowel movement for 15 seconds while semi-recumbent then immediately lay her flat and elevate her legs for 15 seconds. This increases vagal tone slowing AV node conduction and can terminate SVT
option_c: Have her cough forcefully
option_d: Apply ice to her face only
rationale: The modified Valsalva maneuver combines straining to increase intrathoracic pressure with postural change to enhance venous return. Have the patient blow into a 10mL syringe or bear down forcefully for 15 seconds while semi-recumbent at 45 degrees. Immediately lay them flat and elevate their legs for 15 seconds. This sequence increases vagal tone more effectively than traditional Valsalva alone. The vagal response slows conduction through the AV node which can interrupt the reentry circuit that causes most SVT. Success rate is approximately 40-50% for modified versus 17-20% for standard Valsalva. This can be attempted multiple times if unsuccessful.

question: A 70-year-old male with a history of angina is having chest pain. He tells you he took 5 of his nitroglycerin tablets before calling 911. His blood pressure is now 82/54 and he feels dizzy and nauseous. His chest pain has improved to 2 out of 10. What has happened and how should you manage this?
option_a: Give him more nitroglycerin since it is working
option_b: He has excessive hypotension from nitroglycerin overdose. Lay him supine elevate his legs and give an IV fluid bolus to increase preload and blood pressure. Do NOT give any additional nitroglycerin
option_c: His blood pressure is normal
option_d: Give aspirin to counteract the nitroglycerin
rationale: This patient has taken excessive nitroglycerin 5 tablets resulting in significant hypotension from vasodilation. Normal nitroglycerin dosing is up to 3 tablets at 5-minute intervals yet he took 5 likely rapidly. Management of nitroglycerin-induced hypotension includes positioning supine with legs elevated to increase venous return and IV crystalloid bolus to expand intravascular volume and increase preload. The effect of sublingual nitroglycerin is relatively short typically 20-30 minutes so his blood pressure should improve with supportive care. Do not give additional nitroglycerin. Monitor closely and be prepared for complications if his blood pressure does not improve.

question: You are preparing epinephrine for a cardiac arrest patient. Your epinephrine comes in a prefilled syringe containing 1 mg in 10 mL which is what concentration and what is the correct dose for cardiac arrest?
option_a: This is 1:1000 concentration and the dose is 0.1 mg
option_b: This is 1:10000 concentration 0.1 mg per mL and the full 1 mg 10 mL is the correct dose for cardiac arrest given IV or IO
option_c: This is 1:100000 concentration and you should give 0.5 mg
option_d: Give the entire syringe twice for cardiac arrest
rationale: Epinephrine 1 mg in 10 mL equals 0.1 mg per mL which is 1:10000 concentration. This is the standard preparation for IV or IO administration in cardiac arrest. The dose is 1 mg which is the entire 10 mL given IV or IO push every 3-5 minutes during resuscitation. The 1:10000 concentration should not be confused with 1:1000 concentration 1 mg per mL which is used for IM injection in anaphylaxis. Using the wrong concentration by the wrong route could result in giving 10 times too much or too little medication. Always verify concentration before administration.

question: A 58-year-old male with a pacemaker presents after feeling his device fire multiple times. He received about 8 shocks over the past hour. He says each shock felt like being kicked in the chest. He is currently alert and in no distress. The cardiac monitor shows normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm. Vital signs are normal. He has an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator ICD not just a pacemaker. What may be happening and what should you tell him?
option_a: His ICD is malfunctioning and should be turned off with a magnet
option_b: The ICD may be delivering appropriate shocks for actual arrhythmias or inappropriate shocks from lead malfunction or oversensing. He needs urgent device interrogation. If he is currently stable transport for evaluation. If he receives a shock in your presence apply a magnet over the device to inhibit shocks temporarily
option_c: ICDs cannot deliver multiple shocks
option_d: This is normal ICD function
rationale: ICDs deliver shocks to terminate life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. Multiple shocks may indicate actual VT or VF episodes appropriately treated or may indicate device malfunction such as lead fracture causing oversensing or inappropriate detection. Both require urgent evaluation. If the patient is stable and not receiving shocks transport for device interrogation. If he receives shocks while with you especially if the rhythm appears normal a magnet placed over the ICD will temporarily inhibit shock delivery. The magnet does not affect pacing function. He needs urgent cardiology evaluation for device interrogation and possible lead or generator issues.

question: A 6-month-old infant is brought in by parents who report he turns blue during feeding and crying. He was recently diagnosed with a heart murmur. Currently he is irritable and mildly cyanotic with SpO2 of 78% on room air. He is breathing rapidly at 60 per minute with mild retractions. Vital signs show HR 168 RR 60 BP 82/54. What type of condition might this infant have and what should you do if his cyanosis worsens acutely?
option_a: He has pneumonia and needs antibiotics
option_b: This infant likely has a cyanotic congenital heart defect such as Tetralogy of Fallot. The hypercyanotic spell or tet spell treatment is to place him in knee-to-chest position provide oxygen keep him calm and transport. The knee-chest position increases systemic vascular resistance and improves pulmonary blood flow
option_c: Give him albuterol for his breathing
option_d: This is normal for infants
rationale: Cyanosis that worsens with feeding or crying in an infant with known heart murmur strongly suggests cyanotic congenital heart disease. Tetralogy of Fallot is a common cause of hypercyanotic spells or tet spells where right-to-left shunting worsens causing profound cyanosis. The knee-to-chest position increases systemic vascular resistance which reduces right-to-left shunting and improves pulmonary blood flow. Keep the infant calm as crying increases oxygen demand. Provide supplemental oxygen though its effect is limited in right-to-left shunting. IV morphine if available may help reduce hyperpnea and calm the infant. Transport urgently to pediatric cardiac center.

question: A 4-year-old child presents with sudden onset of palpitations and looking pale. His mother says his heart is beating really fast. He has no cardiac history. He is alert but anxious. Vital signs are BP 86/58 HR 240 RR 28 SpO2 98%. The cardiac monitor shows a regular narrow complex tachycardia at 240 bpm. What is the most likely rhythm and what is appropriate management at the AEMT level?
option_a: This is ventricular tachycardia and he needs immediate defibrillation
option_b: This is supraventricular tachycardia SVT which is the most common symptomatic tachyarrhythmia in children. Provide supportive care attempt vagal maneuvers if trained and transport. Ice to the face diving reflex may help in children
option_c: Children cannot have SVT
option_d: His heart rate is normal for a scared child
rationale: SVT is the most common symptomatic tachyarrhythmia in pediatrics. Heart rates of 220-280 bpm are typical in infants and children with SVT. The narrow complex regular rhythm at 240 bpm is consistent with SVT. Vagal maneuvers that work in children include ice or cold wet cloth to the face which triggers the diving reflex. Have the child blow through a straw or blow up a balloon for older cooperative children. These maneuvers may terminate SVT. If the child becomes unstable hypotensive altered or shows signs of heart failure cardioversion is indicated which is an ALS intervention. Provide supportive care and transport.

question: An 85-year-old female presents with weakness and fatigue. She lives alone and has become increasingly debilitated over the past week. She takes multiple medications including metoprolol lisinopril furosemide digoxin and potassium. Her daughter is concerned she may be confused about her medications. Vital signs are BP 94/62 HR 48 irregular RR 16 SpO2 95%. Blood glucose is 92. The cardiac monitor shows an irregular bradycardia with occasional PVCs. She has nausea and reports seeing yellow halos around lights. What toxicity should you consider?
option_a: She is having a stroke
option_b: Consider digoxin toxicity. Classic signs include GI symptoms nausea vomiting cardiac arrhythmias bradycardia PVCs heart blocks and visual disturbances especially yellow-green halos xanthopsia. Her age medications and renal function create high risk for toxicity
option_c: She is just dehydrated
option_d: Her symptoms are from not eating
rationale: Digoxin toxicity is common in elderly patients especially those with renal impairment taking multiple medications or with electrolyte imbalances. The therapeutic window for digoxin is narrow. Classic symptoms include GI manifestations nausea vomiting anorexia cardiac arrhythmias almost any arrhythmia but classically bradycardia AV blocks PVCs and atrial tachycardia with block and visual disturbances particularly yellow-green halos around lights. Her medication regimen with furosemide which can cause hypokalemia increases digoxin toxicity risk. Treatment is supportive with IV access monitoring and transport. Severe toxicity may require digoxin-specific antibody fragments at the hospital.

question: A 78-year-old male fell at home 2 days ago and has had increasing confusion and left-sided weakness since then. He has a history of atrial fibrillation and takes warfarin. His INR was 3.8 at his last check which is above the therapeutic range of 2-3. He has a large bruise on his right forehead from the fall. Vital signs are BP 168/94 HR 78 irregular RR 16 SpO2 97%. He is oriented only to person. What do you suspect and why is his warfarin status important?
option_a: He had a stroke from his atrial fibrillation
option_b: Suspect subdural hematoma. Elderly patients on anticoagulation are at very high risk for intracranial bleeding even from minor trauma. His supratherapeutic INR increases this risk further. The delayed onset of symptoms over days is typical for subdural hematoma. Transport urgently for CT and possible anticoagulation reversal
option_c: His symptoms are from his fall and will improve
option_d: Confusion is normal in elderly patients
rationale: Subdural hematoma results from tearing of bridging veins between the brain and dura. Elderly patients are at high risk because brain atrophy stretches these veins. Anticoagulation particularly with supratherapeutic levels dramatically increases risk and severity. Subdural hematoma typically develops slowly over days to weeks with gradually worsening symptoms unlike epidural hematoma which progresses rapidly. Symptoms include headache confusion personality changes and focal neurological deficits. This patient needs urgent CT imaging and likely anticoagulation reversal with vitamin K and prothrombin complex concentrate or fresh frozen plasma. Transport with close monitoring.

question: You are transporting a patient with chest pain. The cardiac monitor shows ST elevation in the limb leads but you notice there is significant artifact making interpretation difficult. The patient is moving and the ambulance is on a bumpy road. What can you do to improve ECG quality?
option_a: Nothing can be done about motion artifact
option_b: Reduce artifact by ensuring electrodes are properly adhered to clean dry skin. Place electrodes on bony prominences to reduce muscle artifact. Ask the patient to remain still if possible and try to obtain the ECG during a smoother portion of transport. Replace dried-out electrodes
option_c: Switch to a 3-lead instead of 12-lead
option_d: Ignore the artifact and interpret the ECG anyway
rationale: Motion artifact can significantly impair ECG interpretation. Strategies to reduce artifact include proper skin preparation clean dry shaved if needed ensuring good electrode contact replacing old or dried-out electrodes placing electrodes over bony prominences rather than muscle minimizing patient movement and obtaining ECGs during smoother portions of transport if possible. If artifact persists note it in your documentation and relay to the receiving facility that artifact was present. Do not over-interpret a poor quality tracing but also do not dismiss concerning findings. Multiple tracings during transport may help capture a cleaner image.

question: You are monitoring a patient with a history of pacemaker. The cardiac monitor shows regular pacer spikes at 70 per minute each followed by a QRS complex. The patient is feeling well with stable vital signs. Suddenly the monitor shows pacer spikes still at 70 per minute but now every third spike is not followed by a QRS complex. The patient begins to feel dizzy. What is happening?
option_a: The monitor is malfunctioning
option_b: The pacemaker has intermittent failure to capture where some pacer spikes are not resulting in cardiac depolarization. The patient is symptomatic because his effective heart rate has dropped. This is a pacemaker malfunction requiring urgent evaluation
option_c: This is normal pacemaker function
option_d: The electrodes need to be replaced
rationale: Failure to capture occurs when the pacemaker generates an impulse pacer spike but the impulse does not cause myocardial depolarization no QRS following the spike. Intermittent failure to capture means some spikes capture and others do not. This patient's pacemaker is firing at 70 per minute but only 2 of every 3 impulses are capturing giving an effective heart rate of approximately 47 per minute. Causes include lead dislodgement or fracture battery depletion elevated capture threshold from fibrosis or electrolyte abnormalities. The patient is symptomatic from the reduced heart rate. This requires urgent cardiology evaluation and possibly temporary transcutaneous pacing if severely symptomatic.

question: A 55-year-old male presents with palpitations. You place him on the cardiac monitor and see an irregular rhythm with a rate varying between 80-150 bpm. There are no identifiable P waves and the R-R intervals are completely irregular. The QRS complexes are narrow. The patient feels his heart racing then slowing then racing again. What is this rhythm?
option_a: Sinus arrhythmia
option_b: Atrial fibrillation with variable ventricular response. The irregular irregularity with absent P waves and varying R-R intervals is pathognomonic. The narrow QRS indicates conduction through the normal pathway
option_c: Multifocal atrial tachycardia
option_d: Frequent PVCs
rationale: Atrial fibrillation is characterized by irregularly irregular rhythm with absent organized P waves. The atria are fibrillating at 300-600 times per minute creating an irregular baseline without distinct P waves. The AV node conducts some of these impulses to the ventricles in an unpredictable pattern creating the irregularly irregular ventricular response. QRS complexes are narrow unless there is a bundle branch block or aberrant conduction. Symptoms may include palpitations fatigue dyspnea and exercise intolerance. At AEMT level management is supportive with monitoring IV access and transport. Rate control medications are typically ALS or hospital level interventions.

question: You are monitoring a 72-year-old female who had syncope. The cardiac monitor suddenly shows a pause with no ventricular activity for 4.2 seconds before the rhythm returns. The patient says she felt lightheaded during the pause. What does this finding indicate and what should you prepare for?
option_a: A 4-second pause is normal
option_b: This significant pause 4.2 seconds indicates sinus node dysfunction sick sinus syndrome or high-degree AV block. Pauses greater than 3 seconds are concerning for causing syncope. She is at risk for recurrent pauses that could be longer. Prepare for possible cardiac arrest and consider transcutaneous pacing if available and within scope
option_c: Her monitor lead fell off
option_d: This is artifact
rationale: A pause greater than 3 seconds can cause cerebral hypoperfusion leading to syncope or near-syncope as this patient experienced. This suggests significant conduction system disease either sinus node dysfunction failure to generate impulses or AV block failure to conduct impulses. This patient is at risk for longer pauses that could cause cardiac arrest. At AEMT level establish IV access monitor closely and prepare for possible deterioration. If the patient becomes unstable with prolonged pauses or develops cardiac arrest transcutaneous pacing may be indicated. She will likely need permanent pacemaker implantation.

question: A 68-year-old male is having an acute MI. While monitoring him he suddenly develops ventricular fibrillation. He is immediately pulseless and unconscious. What is your immediate action?
option_a: Start CPR for 2 minutes before analyzing the rhythm
option_b: Immediately defibrillate with maximum energy then begin CPR. Do not delay defibrillation for witnessed VF arrest
option_c: Give epinephrine first then defibrillate
option_d: Check for a pulse for 60 seconds to confirm
rationale: For witnessed ventricular fibrillation immediate defibrillation is the priority. Each minute of delay in defibrillation reduces survival by 7-10%. Since you witnessed the arrest and the monitor shows VF defibrillate immediately at maximum energy typically 200 joules biphasic. After the shock immediately begin CPR for 2 minutes before rechecking the rhythm. Do not delay defibrillation to perform CPR in witnessed VF. After the initial shock follow standard VF algorithm with CPR rhythm checks and medications. The exception to immediate defibrillation is unwitnessed arrests where a brief period of CPR before shocking may improve success.

question: A 45-year-old male presents with chest pain after using cocaine approximately 30 minutes ago. He is anxious agitated and diaphoretic. His pupils are dilated. Vital signs are BP 192/118 HR 138 RR 24 SpO2 98%. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST changes. What is different about managing potential cocaine-induced ACS and what medication commonly used in ACS is contraindicated?
option_a: Treat him the same as any ACS patient with aspirin and nitroglycerin
option_b: Cocaine causes coronary vasospasm and direct cardiotoxicity. Aspirin and nitroglycerin are appropriate. However beta-blockers are contraindicated because blocking beta receptors allows unopposed alpha stimulation worsening coronary vasospasm and hypertension
option_c: Give beta-blockers to slow his heart rate
option_d: Cocaine users cannot have heart attacks
rationale: Cocaine causes cardiovascular toxicity through multiple mechanisms including coronary vasospasm increased oxygen demand from tachycardia and hypertension and direct cardiotoxicity. It can cause MI even in young patients with normal coronary arteries. Treatment differs from standard ACS in one critical way: beta-blockers are contraindicated. Beta-blockade leaves alpha-adrenergic stimulation unopposed which can worsen coronary vasospasm and hypertension. Treatment includes aspirin nitroglycerin and benzodiazepines for agitation which reduces catecholamine excess. At AEMT level provide standard ACS care with aspirin and nitroglycerin but note the cocaine use for the receiving team to avoid beta-blockers.

question: A 32-year-old male is experiencing a panic attack according to his girlfriend. He reports chest tightness palpitations shortness of breath numbness in his hands and face and a feeling of impending doom. He is hyperventilating and very anxious. He has had similar episodes before diagnosed as panic attacks. Vital signs are BP 142/88 HR 112 RR 28 SpO2 99%. ECG shows sinus tachycardia. How should you approach this patient?
option_a: Tell him to calm down and breathe into a paper bag
option_b: Take his symptoms seriously. Panic attacks are a diagnosis of exclusion. Perform a complete assessment including ECG and vital signs to rule out cardiac or other medical causes. Provide reassurance but transport for evaluation if any concern for organic cause
option_c: He does not need medical attention for panic attacks
option_d: Give him medication to calm down
rationale: While panic attacks can cause symptoms identical to cardiac emergencies including chest pain palpitations dyspnea and sense of doom they are a diagnosis of exclusion. You cannot definitively diagnose panic attack in the field. Serious conditions like MI PE and arrhythmias can present identically. Perform a thorough assessment including complete history vital signs and ECG. Even if he has a history of panic attacks each episode could be something more serious. Provide calm reassurance and coaching for slow breathing which may help if it is indeed panic. Do not have him breathe into a paper bag as this can worsen hypoxia if there is an underlying medical cause.

question: You treated a patient with chest pain and suspected ACS. You gave aspirin 324 mg nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual x 2 and transported to the hospital. The patient's pain improved from 8 out of 10 to 3 out of 10 with treatment. What are the critical elements to document for this cardiac patient?
option_a: Only document the medications given
option_b: Document symptom onset time and characteristics quality location radiation severity associated symptoms. Document vital signs before and after each intervention. Document medication times doses routes and patient response. Document ECG findings and interpretation. Include cardiac risk factors and relevant medical history
option_c: Document only the chief complaint and disposition
option_d: Documentation is not important for ACS patients
rationale: Thorough documentation for ACS patients is critical for continuity of care and potential legal purposes. Key elements include precise symptom onset time critical for thrombolytic and PCI decisions symptom characteristics OPQRST vital signs at multiple time points especially before and after medications all interventions with exact times doses routes and patient response including pain scale ECG interpretation noting any ST changes relevant history including cardiac risk factors and medications and response to treatment. Document aspirin and nitroglycerin times and effects specifically. If the patient refused any recommended treatment document that discussion. Clear complete documentation ensures the receiving team has all information needed.

question: During transport of your ACS patient his condition suddenly deteriorates. He becomes unresponsive and the monitor shows ventricular fibrillation. You successfully resuscitate him with defibrillation and CPR and he regains a pulse after 4 minutes. What additional documentation is now critical?
option_a: No additional documentation is needed
option_b: Document the exact time of arrest rhythm at arrest all interventions with times including each shock CPR intervals and medications time of ROSC and post-ROSC vital signs and rhythm. Document any changes in neurological status and all treatments provided
option_c: Only document that CPR was performed
option_d: Wait until you reach the hospital to document
rationale: Cardiac arrest documentation requires precise times for all events and interventions. Critical elements include time arrest was recognized initial rhythm exact time and energy of each defibrillation attempt CPR start and stop times noting any interruptions and reasons medication names doses routes and times airway interventions time of ROSC return of spontaneous circulation post-ROSC rhythm vital signs and mental status and any complications. This documentation is essential for quality review determines performance metrics and may be needed for legal purposes. It also guides ongoing care as the duration of arrest affects prognosis and treatment decisions. Document as events occur or immediately after if actively resuscitating.
